variables.content: The proposition: For Fermi liquid parameters $F_l$ (or $F_l/m^{*}$) of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) computed in square periodic simulation cells containing $N$ electrons, the leading systematic finite-size correction arising from long-range correlation effects scales asymptotically as $N^{\gamma}$ with exponent $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., a correction term proportional to $N^{-1/4}$), so that finite-$N$ results can be extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting to the form $F_l(N)=c+aN^{-1/4}$; this scaling is the same as that argued in the theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation finite-size effects (Holzmann et al.).

For the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG), the leading finite-size error in Fermi-liquid-derived quantities such as the angular-momentum Fermi liquid parameters F_l or the Fourier-projected quantities F_l/m^{*} behaves asymptotically as a power law in the finite-system particle number N with exponent -1/4; equivalently,
      \Delta F_l(N) \equiv F_l(N)-F_l(\infty) \propto N^{-1/4},
      so that fitting computed F_l(N) values to the form c + a N^{-1/4} yields an estimate of the thermodynamic-limit intercept c = F_l(\infty). This statement adopts the theoretical asymptotic exponent \gamma = -1/4 predicted for long-range-correlation-dominated finite-size errors.
      Refs. 13,14

For a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (HEG) in a periodic simulation cell containing $N$ electrons, where $E_N$ denotes the computed total energy per electron for the finite-$N$ system and $E_\infty$ denotes the energy per electron in the infinite-system thermodynamic limit, the leading asymptotic finite-size correction to the energy per electron arising from compression of the exchange-correlation hole into the simulation cell and neglected long-range kinetic correlations takes the form $E_N = E_\infty - c/N^{5/4}$ with constant coefficient $c$ (i.e., the dominant finite-size error falls off as $N^{-5/4}$); this $N^{-5/4}$ behavior is the form assumed to dominate at the system sizes used in the present study (up to $N\approx 162$ electrons), so that fitting finite-$N$ fluid-energy data to the single leading-power form $E_N = E_\infty - c/N^{5/4}$ yields the reported estimates of $E_\infty$.
      N. D. Drummond et al., Phys. Rev. B 78, 125106 (2008).

For a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) fluid simulated in a periodically repeated finite simulation cell containing N electrons, let E_N denote the computed energy per electron of that N-electron finite system and let E_∞ denote the energy per electron in the thermodynamic limit; the dominant many-body finite-size error in E_N that arises from compression of the exchange–correlation hole into the simulation cell and from the neglect of long-ranged kinetic-energy correlations falls off as N^{-5/4}, and the infinite-system energy per electron E_∞ may be obtained by fitting finite-system energies to the two-parameter form E_N = E_∞ - c N^{-5/4}, where c and E_∞ are fitting parameters and N is the number of electrons.

For the three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas of number density n>0 and electron mass m define the plasma frequency ω_p = √(4π e^2 n / m) and let N denote the number of electrons in a simulation supercell of volume Ω so that n = N/Ω. Using the random-phase-approximation (RPA) small-wavenumber asymptotic forms
      S(k) ≃ ℏ k^2/(2 m ω_p)
      and
      u(k) ≃ 4π e^2/(ℏ ω_p k^2)
      as k→0, and defining ζ(0) as the sum of the k→0 limits of the potential and kinetic integrands ζ_pot(k)=2π e^2 S(k)/k^2 and ζ_kin(k)=ℏ^2 k^2 u(k)/(4m), substitution of these RPA expressions into ζ(0)/Ω and simplification using ω_p^2 = 4π e^2 N/(m Ω) yields the combined leading finite-size correction for total energy in the homogeneous electron gas:
      Δ_N = ℏ ω_p /(2 N).
      Therefore the dominant long-wavelength finite-size bias in total energy for the homogeneous electron gas can be estimated and removed using only the RPA small-k forms of S(k) and u(k) (or small-k limits inferred from a finite-cell simulation's S_N(G) and u_G), giving a controlled single-simulation correction under the same limiting assumptions that justify the RPA forms.

For the homogeneous electron gas in a finite cubic simulation cell of side length $L$ with Coulomb interaction $v_{q}=4\pi e^{2}/q^{2}$ (Gaussian units), evaluating the restricted small-$q$ integral $\int_{|q_{\alpha}\<\pi/L|}d^{3}q\,...$ together with the frequency integral using the long-wavelength dielectric form $\epsilon(q,\omega)\simeq1-\omega_{p}^{2}/\omega^{2}$ yields a geometric numerical prefactor $C$ multiplying $e^{2}/L$ in the leading finite-size correction $\delta\Sigma(k,0)=C\times(\ldots)$; numerical evaluation of the cubic-domain integral gives $C\simeq1.22$, so that the leading finite-size correction scales as $(1.22\,e^{2}/L)$ times a dimensionless function of $\varepsilon_{k}^{0}-\mu$ and $\omega_{p}$.

For a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas of particle density $n$ and Wigner-Seitz parameter $r_{s}=(\pi n a_{B}^{2})^{-1/2}$ (with Bohr radius $a_{B}=\hbar^{2}/(m e^{2})$), confined to a square periodic simulation cell of linear size $L$ and area $V=L^{2}$ containing $N$ electrons (so that $n=N/V$), define the finite-size correction
      $$
      \Delta_{N}=\int_{-\pi/L}^{\pi/L}\frac{d^{2}\mathbf{q}}{(2\pi)^{2}}\;\delta(q),
      $$
      with
      $$
      \delta(q)=u_{q}\bigl[1-S(q)\bigr]-n\,u_{q}^{2}S(q),
      $$
      where $u_{q}$ is the Fourier transform of the many-body Jastrow potential, $S(q)$ is the static structure factor, $n$ is the particle density, and the integral is taken over the small rectangular patch of wavevectors with components $|q_{x}|\le\pi/L$, $|q_{y}|\le\pi/L$. The discontinuity (renormalization factor) of the momentum distribution at the Fermi surface in the thermodynamic limit, $Z_{k_{F}}^{\infty}$, is related to its finite-$N$ value $Z_{k_{F}}^{N}$ by the multiplicative relation
      $$
      Z_{k_{F}}^{\infty}\simeq Z_{k_{F}}^{N}\,e^{-\Delta_{N}},
      $$
      where, using the long-wavelength approximations for small $q$,
      $$
      2n u_{q}\simeq-1+\sqrt{1+\frac{2n v_{q}}{\varepsilon_{q}}},\qquad v_{q}=\frac{2\pi e^{2}}{q},\qquad \varepsilon_{q}=\frac{\hbar^{2}q^{2}}{2m},
      $$
      the leading asymptotic scaling for large $N$ with $L=\sqrt{N/n}$ is
      $$
      \Delta_{N}\simeq\left(\frac{\pi r_{s}^{2}}{4N}\right)^{-1/4}\qquad\text{for }N\rightarrow\infty.
      $$
      Hence, under these long-wavelength approximations the dominant finite-size error in the renormalization factor $Z$ decays anomalously slowly as $N^{-1/4}$ rather than as an integer power $1/N$.

In the enhancement-factor model $F_{xc}(l)=1+(\alpha+\beta l)/(1+\gamma l)$, the optimized value $\alpha=-0.0007$ gives $F_{xc}(0)\approx 1$. Because the homogeneous electron gas limit is defined by $s^{2}=0$ and $l=0$, with exchange-correlation energy density $e_{xc}^{\mathrm{HEG}}(r_s)$, the fitted model therefore recovers the homogeneous-electron-gas limit to within a very small deviation and does not introduce a substantial spurious correction for a spatially uniform electron gas.

Consider a periodic crystalline solid with unit-cell linear size $a$ and an $n\times n\times n$ supercell of linear dimension $L=n a$. If long-range correlation pair interactions decay asymptotically as $C_6/d^6$ (London dispersion tails) at large interatomic separations $d$, then the leading finite-size correction to the per-unit-cell correlation energy obtained by integrating these pairwise tails over the volume exterior to a sphere of radius $d$ scales as $L^{-3}$. Therefore for large $n$ the per-unit-cell correlation energy can be extrapolated as $E_X^{n} = E_X^{n\to\infty} + S (n a)^{-3}$, and this $L^{-3}$ extrapolation is applicable when long-range pairwise $C_6$ behavior dominates finite-size errors and many-body finite-size contributions or slow low-energy collective excitations are subleading for the system under study.

With definitions as in P2 (segment length $L$, correlator $g_x=\sin(k_F x)/(\pi x)$, $s(x)=\min(x,L)$), the proposition is: in the large-$L$ asymptotic expansion of $2\sum_{x=1}^{\infty}[g_x]^2 s(x)$ there is no term proportional to $L^{-1}$; the first non-logarithmic finite-size correction is of order $L^{-2}$ (possibly with oscillatory $L^{-2}$ contributions), so the coefficient of $L^{-1}$ vanishes exactly in the performed summation.
